title: Concurrent Exhibitions at List Gallery
description:
Join us for two concurrent exhibitions at the List Gallery: Paper Journeys: Pop-up Books and Constructions by Colette Fu and Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose: Selected East Asian Works from the Swarthmore College Art Collection. Together, these exhibitions explore current and historical East Asian works and themes, such as culture and identity, travel and its transformations, and the importance of diverse narratives. Paper Journeys features mixed-media works by Colette Fu, a Philadelphia-based artist who is known for her paper constructions that explore her Nuosu Yi ancestry and the cultural traditions of diverse ethnic minority groups in China. Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose features works from Swarthmore's College Art Collection and highlights concerns explored by East Asian artists from the 14th century to the mid-twentieth century. These interrelated exhibitions were co-curated and organized by Andrea Packard, Director of the Swarthmore College Art Collection and Curator of the List Gallery; Sara Hesdon Buehler, Art Collection Manager; and Susan Eberhard, Visiting Assistant Professor, Art History.

The exhibitions will be on view in the List Gallery from March 3 through April 19, 2026.
Colette Fu will give a talk about her work on Thursday, March 19 at 4:30 PM in the Lang Performing Arts Center Cinema; an opening reception will follow in the List Gallery from 5:30 to 7:00 PM.

title: Anatomy of a Shadow Economy: Body Snatching and the Making of American Medicine
description:
Anatomy of a Shadow Economy: Body Snatching and the Making of American Medicine


 

Kimberly Williams- Professor & Chair of Anthropology, Temple University

Scheuer Room, Kohlberg Hall

April 1, 4:30-6:00 p.m.

 

Description:

This is the first in a series of events on the renaming of Old Science Hall. 

The emergence of a clandestine cadaver trade in the late 18th and early 19th centuries was a foundational element of American medical education. Focusing on urban centers such as Philadelphia, this talk traces how chronic shortages of legally sanctioned bodies gave rise to an informal economy of “resurrectionists,” intermediaries, and anatomists. This talk reconstructs the organization, economy, and social geography of this trade, highlighting its reliance on the bodies of the poor, the institutionalized, and racialized populations. It situates the cadaver market within broader histories of urban inequality, property, and the governance of the dead. An examination of the contemporaneous public resistance reveals not only moral outrage but competing claims over the value of personhood, the integrity of the body, and postmortem rights. By foregrounding the shadow economy that sustained medical training, this talk reframes the development of American medicine as deeply entangled with coercion, marginality, and contested forms of value, offering a critical lens on the foundations of modern anatomical knowledge.

Note: This talk confronts difficult topics surrounding the illegal, immoral, and unethical trade of bodies, especially the bodies of marginalized communities.

Kimberly Williams is a bioarchaeologist and professor and chair of anthropology at Temple University whose scholarship rests at the intersection of field archaeology and skeletal biology. She currently serves as a consultant on the Old Science Hall Renaming Task Force.

title: P&A Colloquium: Lamiya Mowla, Wellesley
description:
Spring 2026  Physics and Astronomy Colloquium

Friday, April 3, 2026, 12:45 pm, Cunniff Lecture Hall (SCI 199)

Lamiya Mowla
Assistant Professor 
Physics and Astronomy
Wellesley College

Observing the Birth and Growth of Galaxies since the Big Bang

How did galaxies like our Milky Way form? To answer this question, astronomers look back to the early years of the Universe, when the first galaxies were just beginning to take shape. Using the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), we can now study galaxies from a time when the Universe was only about 5 percent of its current age. In this talk, I will share observations of distant galaxies nicknamed the “Firefly Sparkle,” whose light has traveled more than 13 billion years to reach us. This young galaxy is forming from compact, intense star clusters and offers a glimpse into the early stages of growth that eventually led to galaxies like our own Milky Way. I will also present new results from extending this work to more galaxies, allowing us to trace how star clusters and galaxies grew together across cosmic history.

Finally, I will highlight projects being built with Wellesley students that broaden access to astronomy research and its history. These include J-HIVE, a public data and visualization platform for galaxies observed with JWST, and Mirror, an initiative that preserves the erased histories of women astronomers through a digital archive and a permanent exhibit at Whitin Observatory in celebration of its 125 years.

BIO: Prof. Mowla is an Assistant Professor of Astronomy at Wellesley College. She earned her Ph.D. from Yale University in 2020 under the mentorship of Prof. Pieter van Dokkum. Before joining Wellesley, she was a Dunlap Postdoctoral Fellow at the Dunlap Institute for Astronomy and Astrophysics, University of Toronto. She is originally from Dhaka, Bangladesh, and is an inaugural Associate Member of the Center for Astronomy, Space Science, and Astrophysics at Independent University, Bangladesh—the first astronomy research center in the country.

The colloquium will begin promptly at 12:45 p.m. in Science Center 199.

A pizza lunch will be available at 12:25 p.m.

title: Building Trauma-Informed Campus Support for Survivors (Immigrant-Informed)
description: This is a collaboration with the Nationalities Service Center (NSC),  a Philadelphia nonprofit that provides trauma-informed services to immigrant and refugee communities, including survivors of violence and other forms of trauma. The event delivers a trauma-informed campus support workshop that equips students, faculty, and staff with practical tools for responding to disclosures of violence and connecting survivors to appropriate resources. This programming is especially timely given the recent increase in Title IX–related concerns across campus and the corresponding need for clearer, more survivor-centered support practices in everyday campus spaces. While the workshop is open to all survivors of violence, it includes an immigrant-informed lens to address cultural and structural barriers (e.g., language access, stigma, and fear of systems) that can shape help-seeking and safety.

title: Writing Cross Cultures, Working Between Languages: The Translational Prism of Taiwan
description:
This talk explores the intricate histories and human connections embedded in contemporary Taiwanese literature—works that strive to reclaim forbidden memories and navigate the identity of a multilingual,
immigrant society. Using translation as a "prism," we will examine the diverse facets of Taiwanese culture and the power of literary translation to capture the subtle nuances of shifting linguistic practices.

Featuring Lin King Lin King is an acclaimed writer and translator from Taiwan. A 2018 winner of the PEN/Robert J. Dau Short Story Prize, she reached a career milestone in 2024 as a winner of the National Book Award for Translated Literature. Joining us to discuss the challenges and promises of the craft, she will focus on three of her major translated works: Taiwan Travelogue: A Novel, The Boy from Clearwater, and A Perfect Day to Put Your Head into the Oven.

Sponsored by Modern Languages and Literatures (Chinese, Japanese and Russian Sections), Asian Studies, Comparative Literature and Global Studies Contact: Claire Li; cli3@swarthmore.edu

title: GEO Cape Town Consortium Information Session
description:
The Consortium Program, established in 2003, offers students of Macalester, Pomona, and Swarthmore Colleges (and their affiliated institutions) the opportunity to study the environment and complex, multi-cultural society of South Africa against a background of globalization and rapid technological change. The program takes place at the University of Cape Town, a world-class institution where professors, in consultation with those from the consortium colleges, have developed a curriculum that meets the high academic standards of our schools while at the same time facilitating the challenge of living and studying in a foreign country. The program is hosted by the Department of Environmental and Geographical Science (EGS) at the University of Cape Town (UCT), which has an internationally respected student-faculty team with strong areas of interest in issues such as environmental and climate change, Global Information Systems, urban and agricultural development, and conservation of biodiversity. Cape Town itself is a breathtakingly beautiful city nestled against Table Mountain at the southwestern tip of Africa.

 

This is a spring only program.

 

Join us for Q&A with Prof. Carr Everbach, Andie D'Agostino, Assistant Director in GEO, and returned study abroad students.

title: Pomona: A Play Directed by Montana Hamel
description:
POMONA: a play directed by Montana Hamel

 

• Friday, April 10th, 8pm

Martin 013 (Production Studio)

 

• Saturday, April 11th, 6pm and 9pm

Martin 013 (Production Studio)

 

• Sunday, April 12th, 8pm

Martin 013 (Production Studio)


Written by British playwright Alistair McDowall and directed by Montana Hamel ‘26, POMONA is a witty, macabre meditation on the gruesome underbelly of the corrupt society we inhabit, which illuminates the sacrifice and humanity that bind us together. As a one-act play, it confronts the viewer with a non-chronological narrative that highlights the tragedy of each character’s connection to Pomona, a concrete hole in the middle of the city that is home to the unseen horrors of the elite who profit from black market trade. For only three nights this April, come solve the puzzle of Pomona for yourself, and watch the characters of the play fall victim to the loop of fate as they navigate their connections to Pomona, the law, the Great Old Ones, and themselves. 

 

Free and open to the public. Audience will not need tickets, first-come, first-served seating. The play is 1 hr and 45 mins.

title: "Literary Life Histories: Reclaiming Reading and Living the Story" Book Talk
description:
Please join us for a book talk with Drs. Susan Browne, Valarie G. Lee and Marjorie E. Madden.

Literary Life Histories: Reclaiming Reading and Living the Story speaks to literacy instruction and practices across a large span that includes upper elementary through university classrooms. The book looks at the intersection of comprehension, engagement and culture in literacy education.

Through an innovative framework of literary life histories — Read, Interpret, Script, Perform — students explore the intertextuality of character and culture in texts and learn to think more deeply and compassionately about the world. This book shows middle and high school teachers and college instructors how to engage students in immersive reading using critical, multicultural texts that ultimately culminates in rich dramatic performances.

title: Judd Kessler Lecture
description:
Department of Economics

Judd B. Kessler, Ph.D.
Howard Marks Professor
Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania 

Lucky by Design
Book Talk

Wharton economist Judd Kessler discusses his new book, “Lucky by Design: The Hidden Economics You Need to Get More of What You Want.”

Professor Kessler pulls back the curtain on the hidden markets that determine who gets what in everyday life. Unlike familiar markets that you might learn about in your economics class — where what we get depends on how much we’re willing to pay — hidden markets do not rely on prices: you can’t buy your way into a better position. Instead, what you receive hinges on the rules by which the market operates, and the choices you make in them.

What are the tricks for getting reservations at the hottest restaurants, live-event tickets, job offers, and spots in elite preschools and selective colleges? What about finding a soulmate on a dating app or receiving a life-saving organ transplant? How can policymakers design these markets better?

Professor Kessler has spent a career studying and designing these very markets. Now, he reveals insights about how they work, how to maneuver in them, and how to tip the scales in your favor.

Complimentary copies of Professor Kessler's book will be available on a first-come, first-served basis.

title: Acting Up in Hopeless Times Sager Symposium
description:
Acting Up in Hopeless Times

Sager Symposium

April 14

Kohlberg Hall, Scheuer Room

 

Schedule of Events


  • 9:55-11:10 a.m. - Panel 1:  Creating Protest/Resistance from Public Panic (Marlon Bailey, Jamal Batts, Salonee Bhanam) + moderated Q&A
  • 2:40-3:55 p.m. - Panel 2: Queer Historical Imagination in Crisis (Debra Levine, Dahlia Li, Olivia Polk) + moderated Q&A 
  • 7:00-9:30 p.m. - Activist Teach In and Q&A with ACT UP member, Debra Levine

Please RSVP for this event by 3:00 p.m. on April 7th.

Acting Up in Hopeless Times convenes an intergenerational group of public intellectuals, community historians, activists, and scholars to consider the ongoing queer legacies of the 1980’s AIDS pandemic and ACT UP activism. This symposium puts the politically-dire situation of 2026 for LGBTQ+ individuals in continuity with the political histories begun and continued in the 1980’s AIDS epidemic. By framing LGBTQ+ activism, culture, and community as the direct result of political crisis, rather than its pre-emption or remedy, this symposium aims to catalyze a renewal of 1) queer theory and historiography within scholarly and public discourses and 2) forms of queer civic and political life that have faltered since the discontinuities of knowledge transfer created by COVID-19. This event is free, accessible and open to the public.

To see full event details, please visit our website. 

To view the AccessAble USA guide for the Scheuer Room of Kohlberg Hall please click here. To view AccessAble USA for Swarthmore College, please click this link. 

For directions, parking, and a campus map, please visit https://www.swarthmore.edu/visit-swarthmore.

For questions, please contact kgomezz1@swarthmore.edu.

title: Arabic Language Table
description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. It fosters spontaneous speech, reinforces vocabulary in context, and encourages cultural exploration through discussions on music, films, and current events. By creating a sense of community, it connects students across proficiency levels, motivating them to participate and apply their skills in real-world interactions. This informal setting helps bridge classroom learning with practical use, preparing students for study abroad, internships, and conversations with native speakers. Ultimately, it enhances fluency, cultural awareness, and a deeper appreciation for the Arabic language.

title: Inclusive Excellence Lunch & Learns
description:
Lunch and Learn: Moving Through Conflict with Clarity and Care

 


Please join us this academic year for the Lunch and Learn Series 2025-2026. Focusing on Healing and Re-building Community, we have invited Creative Praxis to Swarthmore to guide us through this year's series. 

 

Creative Praxis is an arts-based, healing-centered, and joy-filled training organization. Their programs are designed to inspire and connect people, fostering personal and community transformation. With over 60 years of combined experience, their facilitators are experts in interactive, transformational facilitation. The founder, Nia Eubanks-Dixon, is certified in restorative practices, trauma healing, and de-escalation techniques.

 

September 11th - Moving Through Conflict with Clarity and Care  - An interactive session that challenges the fear of conflict for growth and connection. Learn culturally relevant strategies to navigate conflict with empathy, compassion, and clarity. When conflict is approached with intention, honesty, and care, we strengthen relationships and build healthier communities.
location: Hormel/Nguyen Intercultural Center at Sproul Hall Room 201 Dome Room

title: On the Air in Changing Times – A German Correspondent in the U.S
description:
Please join us for a talk by Christina Eck, U.S. Radio Correspondent for the German Press Agency DPA in Washington, DC. Christina has also worked as a freelance correspondent and producer for German Public Radio ARD in Washington, DC, as a Reporter, Anchor, and Editor for Antenne Bayern Radio in Munich Germany, and a senior Editor, Anchor, and International Broadcaster for the German Service of Voice of America in Washington, DC.
Ms. Eck has been busy covering US-news for German radio for more than 30 years and she will take you on a little time travel, focusing on the continuity and change of news and journalism over the decades.
This event is sponsored by the German Studies section of the Department of Modern Languages and Literatures and the Department of Film and Media Studies.

From African drumming to Tibetan singing bowls sound has been used to guide and heal the body for centuries! All you have to do is simply rest, be present, and allow your body to drift along a beautiful soundscape journey.

Part of the 2nd Annual Be Well Community Wellness Day!

title: SSQL Workshop: Data Visualization in R
description: This workshop will focus on how to visually explore and communicate your data in R. You will learn key techniques for creating effective charts and graphs, choosing appropriate visualizations for different types of data, and interpreting the patterns you see. We will cover essential packages like ggplot2, customizing plots, combining multiple plots, and best practices for making your visuals clear and informative. By the end of the session, you'll be able to generate publication-quality graphics and use data visualization as a powerful tool for analysis and storytelling.

Teams are assessed on overall business idea, creativity, and quality of their pitch and are rewarded through cash prizes.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: The Harvard School of Meaning- Centered Medical Anthropology: Reflections After 50 Years
description:
The Harvard School of Meaning-Centered Medical Anthropology: Reflections After 50 Years


 

WHEN: Tuesday, April 21, 2026, 4:30 pm

WHERE: Science Center 101

 

Why should meaning-centered Medical Anthropology matter for undergraduates, social scientists, and educated people globally? This talk uses the story of Harvard's five decades of medical anthropological teaching, research and practice to illustrate why a meaning-centered approach to experience is so vital for our times. That approach creates a framework for the practical application of wisdom derived from the human sciences and puts it to work to reimagine our social condition and develop more availing approaches and practices. This talk is dedicated to a former student and leader of the Harvard program: Paul Farmer, whose brilliance and moral passion informed our emphasis on accompaniment, human rights, and integration of the moral, the emotional and the practical.

title: “What’s in a (Building’s) Name?”
description:
“What’s in a (Building’s) Name?”


 

Scheuer Room, Kohlberg Hall

April 22, 4:30-6:00 p.m.

 

Lisa Alvarez-Cohen, Vice Provost for Academic Planning, UC Berkeley

Kim Cassidy ’85, H’25, Ninth President, Bryn Mawr College

Cat Norris, Chair, Renaming Task Force and Moderator  

 

Description:

This is the third in a series of events on the renaming of Old Science Hall. 

Lisa Alvarez-Cohen, an environmental engineer whose research focuses on the application of microbiological processes for the elimination of environmental contaminants, was named Berkeley’s vice provost for academic planning in 2018. Last year, Berkeley chose an Indigenous name for a campus building and consulted with Indigenous people in the process.

 

Kimberly Wright Cassidy ’85, H’25, joined Bryn Mawr’s psychology faculty in 1993 and served as its ninth president from 2013-2024. During her tenure as president, she oversaw the renaming of Bryn Mawr’s library.  

 

Cat Norris is Associate Dean of the Faculty for Academic Programs and Research and an associate professor of psychology. She joined Swarthmore’s faculty in 2013.

title: Senior Lang Scholar Presentations
description:
Launched in the early 80s at Swarthmore College by Eugene M. Lang ’38, the Lang Opportunity Scholarship Program selects up to six members of the sophomore class each year and provides them with an array of undergraduate and graduate benefits – including a $12k project grant.

To date, LOS has cultivated 100s of Lang Scholars who have partnered with 100s more communities on projects all over the world. Join the LOS Class of 2026 to hear about their inspiring projects! They are:


  • Mya Chuluundorj's project, “Story-Capturers: The Voices of the Land,” is a digital archive preserving the cultural heritage of Mongolian rural communities, engaging students against climate change and urbanization.  
  • Lena Habtu created a documentary with Human Rights First Ethiopia highlighting Tigrayans’ experiences during the 2020-2022 conflict.
  • Prerna Karmacharya’s project seeks to decrease the underrepresentation of blind and visually impaired individuals in STEM fields like robotics in partnership with Perkins School for the Blind. 
  • Steven Mukum’s project, Rising Above Water, addresses internal displacement in Dangbo, Benin, caused by climate-related flooding through training in sustainable agriculture and providing flood-resistant infrastructure in partnership with OJECC Bénin.
  • Billy Wu’s project, Acquired Tomorrows, reduces stigma and increases access to mental health with project partner, Home for the Disabled Kunshan and ShangXiang Counseling.

title: Senior Case 2026
description:
The Senior Showcase provides a space where the Swarthmore community comes together to learn from senior projects across all three divisions. Participating students each have three minutes and one slide to present their work. Why only three minutes when our students could spend hours sharing the details of their projects? Most people will not take more than three minutes to listen and understand a student's work; therefore, students need to learn both how to distill their work into a three-minute sound bite and how to explain their work to a more general audience. Three minutes also allows for more participation from interested seniors.

title: Exhibition: Artists' Books: Pop-Up!
description: This exhibition features innovative and diverse pop-up books from the Book Arts & Private Press Collection. The works on view demonstrate how contemporary book artists are using the book structure to inform their art. Of special interest are works by Philadelphia-based artist Colette Fu, whose work explores her Nuosu Yi ancestry and the cultural traditions of diverse ethnic minority communities in China. Fu will be teaching an introduction to pop-up book structures in McCabe Library on March 28.

description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. title: Arabic Language Table
description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. It fosters spontaneous speech, reinforces vocabulary in context, and encourages cultural exploration through discussions on music, films, and current events. By creating a sense of community, it connects students across proficiency levels, motivating them to participate and apply their skills in real-world interactions. This informal setting helps bridge classroom learning with practical use, preparing students for study abroad, internships, and conversations with native speakers. Ultimately, it enhances fluency, cultural awareness, and a deeper appreciation for the Arabic language.

title: A Talk by Jeremy Johnson, Cultural Education Director, Delaware Tribe of Indians
description:
A Talk by Jeremy Johnson, Cultural Education Director, Delaware Tribe of Indians


 

Scheuer Room, Kohlberg Hall

April 29, 4:30-6:00 p.m.

 

Description:

This is the fourth and final event in a series on the renaming of Old Science Hall.

 

Jeremy Johnson is the cultural education director of the Delaware Tribe of Indians, based in Bartlesville, Okla. He is Lenape, Absentee Shawnee, and Peoria. Prior to his current role, he served as assistant chief of the Delaware Tribe. A lifelong educator, he is committed to preserving and revitalizing Lenape culture and language for the future generations of his tribe. 

 

All events are sponsored by the Swarthmore College President’s Office.

title: Peter van de Kamp Observatory Open House
description:
The Physics and Astronomy department hosts a telescope open house on the second Tuesday of each month at the Peter van de Kamp Observatory on the roof of the Science Center. Directions are available at www.swarthmore.edu/peter-van-de-kamp-observatory/directions.

The open house hours are 8:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. during standard time and 9:00 p.m. to 10:00 p.m. during daylight savings time.

If the weather is bad, the open house will be cancelled and an announcement will be posted on the Physics and Astronomy department website and the College calendar. Check https://www.swarthmore.edu/physics-astronomy on the day of the open house if the weather is questionable

Open to the public.
location: Science Center Peter Van de Kamp Observatory, Science Center Observatory Computer Room
